coredata.dc:title: Sensing formic acid with a water-based chitosan lanthanide luminescence film
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Xu
Chen, Xi
Yu, Xudong
Yang, Yifan
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and selective formic acid sensor could be useful in detecting this corrosive acid in industrial, medical, and environmental settings. Herein, we present a film for detection of formic acid. We introduced chitosan as a film-forming factor to creatively overcome the problem that chitosan dissolves in acids. A carboxylic acid-carbon dot solution was used to replace the strong acid solution and prepare a luminescent film in which the color changed from green to blue when it was exposed to formic acid. Integrated data analysis of the film was used to distinguish vapor from formic acid/water solutions at different concentrations and with a detection limit of 0.01% v/v. The selectivity of the sensor was also investigated. After fumigation with ethanol and other common solvents, there was no response similar to that for formic acid. It is believed that films based on lanthanide complexes and chitosan could prevent corrosion and injury from formic acid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Formic acid
Water-soluble chitosan
Luminescence
Lanthanide
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The selectivity influences of formic acid (FA)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) CDs and (B) Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS. (C-D) EDX elemental mapping of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS. (E) TG curves of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS.
FTIR spectra of the CDs, CS, CDs-CS, TTA-Si, Tb(TTA-Si)3, and the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS film in its initial state and after fumigation with formic acid.
(A) UV spectra of CDs, CDs-CS, and Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS. (B) Excitation and (C) emission luminescence spectra of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS.
(A) Luminescence responses of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS to varying FA concentrations. (B) Gas sensing responses of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS film upon exposure to formic acid/water vapors.
Luminescence emission intensities of the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CD-CS film in the presence of VOCs.

head.ce:title: Sensing formic acid with a water-based chitosan lanthanide luminescence film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment Science & Engineering, Hebei University of Science and Technology 70 Yuhua Rd, Shijiazhuang 050018, Hebei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment Science & Engineering, Hebei University of Science and Technology 70 Yuhua Rd, Shijiazhuang 050018, Hebei, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Control on Surface and Interface, College of Science Hebei University of Science and Technology 70 Yuhua Rd, Shijiazhuang 050018, Hebei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Control on Surface and Interface
Hebei University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Photoelectric Control on Surface and Interface, College of Science Hebei University of Science and Technology 70 Yuhua Rd, Shijiazhuang 050018, Hebei, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and selective formic acid sensor could be useful in detecting this corrosive acid in industrial, medical, and environmental settings. Herein, we present a film for detection of formic acid. We introduced chitosan as a film-forming factor to creatively overcome the problem that chitosan dissolves in acids. A carboxylic acid-carbon dot solution was used to replace the strong acid solution and prepare a luminescent film in which the color changed from green to blue when it was exposed to formic acid. Integrated data analysis of the film was used to distinguish vapor from formic acid/water solutions at different concentrations and with a detection limit of 0.01% v/v. The selectivity of the sensor was also investigated. After fumigation with ethanol and other common solvents, there was no response similar to that for formic acid. It is believed that films based on lanthanide complexes and chitosan could prevent corrosion and injury from formic acid.

ce:section.ce:para: Formic acid is the simplest carboxylic acid derivative. It may also lead to diffusion of acidic substances into the atmosphere and acidify rainwater; hence, it plays an important role in atmospheric chemistry [1]. It also affects aqueous phase chemistry, as it is highly acidic and affects the pH of water. Health hazards mainly cause skin and mucous membrane irritation. After contact, conjunctivitis, eyelid edema, rhinitis, and bronchitis can occur, and serious complications can cause acute chemical pneumonia [2]. When ingested orally, concentrated formic acid can corrode the mucosa of the mouth and digestive tract, causing vomiting, diarrhea and gastrointestinal bleeding, and even death due to acute renal failure or respiratory failure. Skin contact may cause inflammation and ulcers and occasionally allergic reactions. Formic acid is toxic because it inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase [3]. Because of these harmful effects and the uses of formic acid, monitoring its concentration is extremely important. It is highly pungent and corrosive, and it has a permissible exposure limit (U.S. OSHAPEL) of 5 ppm [4]. The detection methods reported for formic acid include chromatography, microwave technology and electrochemical methods [5–7]. Direct detection of formic acid with optical signals is a relatively simple, economical and convenient method that is suitable for a variety of field conditions. An inexpensive, real-time formic acid vapor sensor would protect worker health and limit formic acid corrosion on metal components.

Chitosan (CS) is a biomolecular material with good biocompatibility and chemical processing properties. It is the second most abundant polymer after cellulose. The unique properties of chitosan are mainly derived from its amino groups [8]. They are protonated to –NH3 + in aqueous acidic solvents, and this renders the polymer soluble. Most soft chitosan materials dissolve in strong acids such as sulfuric acid or phosphoric acid, the preparation of which affects the environment [9]. C-dots (CDs) are a new family of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm, and they are known for their unique luminescence properties. The pH values of carbon dots differ depending on the materials used to make them [10,11]. Dissolution of chitosan in an acidic aqueous solution of CDs would constitute to environmentally friendly application of chitosan. This study will enrich the knowledge of environmentally friendly solvents. The surfaces of CDs are rich in functional groups that provide abundant binding sites for specific receptors. The biocompatibility and biodegradability of chitosan perfectly fit the need for detection without causing secondary harm to the user.

Luminescent film sensors have many advantages, such as reusability, ease of manufacture, strong integration ability and real-time online detection capability, and they show great advantages in detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs). The film in which the analyte interacts with the sensing unit produces a luminescence signal change and is easy to carry, easy to preserve, and easy to apply for VOC detection [12–14]. Wu [5] presented a nanofilm-based fluorescent sensor for detection of formic acid vapor. By changing the concentration of the building blocks, the fluorescence intensity of the nanofilm can be easily adjusted, and the sensing performance can be optimized. Using a nanofilm, a fluorescent sensor with a wide response range (4.4 ppt − 4400 ppm) for real-time and online detection of formic acid vapor was built. With the sensor, a trace amount (0.01%) of formic acid in petroleum ether (60–90 °C) can be detected within 3 s. Tammanoon [4] conducted a detailed study on the sensitivity and selectivity of Zn-substituted SnO2 nanoparticles for formic acid (HCOOH) detection. The test results showed that the SnO2 sensing layer doped with 0.2 wt% Zn offered an optimal response with a response time of 1.4 s toward 1000 ppm HCOOH at the optimum working temperature of 350 °C. Complex preparation methods and precise detection instruments are not conducive to industrial monitoring of formic acid. Moonrinta [1] et al. used yogurt as a raw material to prepare carbon dots and used them to detect formic acid with a detection limit of 7.3% v/v. The use of yogurt as a raw material limited the repeatability of the experiment.

In this study, the mixed-lanthanide luminescent film with chitosan were readily synthesized. It was designed with water-soluble CDs, lanthanide complexes and chitosan. The solubilization of chitosan with a carbon dot solution can contribute to the design of environmentally friendly materials. In consideration of this, aiming at formic acid, Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS (The C-dots and chitosan abbreviation combined with lanthanide complexes were incorporated into the name of the new material) is further developed as a luminescent probe, our research indicates the practical application of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS for formic acid detection.

Chitosan with a deacetylation degree of 85% was obtained from J&K and used as received. TbCl3·6H2O, 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane, 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 2-thenoyltrifluoroacetone (TTA) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). All solvents and reagents were used without further purification.

UV − Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV − visible Agilent Cray 100 spectrometer with a quartz cuvette. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer over a wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1 with the KBr pellet technique. Luminescence (excitation and emission) spectra were obtained on a Hitachi F-4600 phosphorimeter using a xenon lamp as the excitation source. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were carried out on a Perkin Elmer Pyris Diamond thermal analyzer at a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 under a nitrogen atmosphere. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and chemical mapping were recorded with a Tecnai G2 F20 system (FEI Co. U.S.A.) operated with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

First, 0.033 mg of 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid was dissolved into 10 mL of water and mixed ultrasonically for 10 min. The homogeneous solution was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (30 mL). After being heated at 180 °C for 72 h, the solution was dialyzed against water for 1 day.

2-Thenoyltrifluoroacetone (3 mmol) was dissolved in anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (20 mL), and 3 mmol of NaH was added and reacted for 2 hours. Then, 20 mL of a tetrahydrofuran solution containing 3 mmol of 3-isocyanatopropyltriethoxysilane was added slowly. After refluxing the reaction for 12 hours, the solvent was removed by evaporation, and the viscous, yellow, oily product, TTA-Si, was obtained.

The prepared precursor, TTA-Si, was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran, and the appropriate amount of TbCl3·6H2O was added. The solution was heated to reflux for 24 hours, and then the solvent was removed by vacuum distillation to obtain a yellow powder.

CS (Chitosan, 0.15 g) was dissolved into 15 ml of the dialyzed CDs solution with ultrasonic mixing for 10 min. An aqueous solution of Tb(TTA-Si)3 was added and ultrasonically mixed for 30 min, and a viscous solution was obtained.

Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS (0.05 g) was added dropwise to 10 mL of deionized water to obtain a clear solution. A film was formed by dripping the solution onto glass microslides, and the water was evaporated at 40 °C in air. The preparation and formic acid detection of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity influences of formic acid (FA)
ce:section.ce:para: To assess this probe’s selectivity, interferents, including several VOCs (such as ethanol, n-hexane, methanol, toluene, benzene, ethyl acetate and trichloromethane), were added. A small beaker containing the VOC solution was placed in a large, upturned beaker. After the film was placed in the beaker, it was closed, and the reaction was timed. After 10 minutes, the film was removed for testing. (Ambient temperature: room temperature (25℃)).

Characterization of the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS was monodisperse with a snow-like structure, and the average particle diameter was 50 nm (Fig. 2 B). High-resolution TEM images showed that the hybrid material contained many small needle-like structures. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy with chemical mapping showed a homogeneous distribution of Tb in the hybrid material (Fig. 2C, 2D). The morphology of the CDs is shown in Fig. 2A, which demonstrates the uniform and monodisperse nature of the CDs (average diameter = 3.7 nm).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded for the CDs, CS, CDs-CS, TTA-Si, Tb(TTA-Si)3, and the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS film in its initial state and after fumigation with formic acid. The peak at 1688 cm−1 corresponding to the C = O stretching vibration (ν C=O) of the CDs disappeared in the spectra of CDs-CS and Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS, illustrating coordination of the carboxylate groups with Tb3+ in Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS [15]. B − O peak was observed at 1313 cm−1 for the CDs, and it also appeared weakly in the spectrum of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS, which indicated that boric acid did not participate in coordination [16]. The spectrum of CS had a broad OH stretching absorption at 3458 cm−1. CS has –NH3 + stretching vibrations near 2900 cm−1, which are enhanced by interactions with the polyanions [9]. The amino groups in CS were bound to H+ in the CD solution and produced a positively charged polyelectrolyte. Intermolecular hydrogen bonds were broken and the material dissolved. Amide I and amide II absorption peaks appeared between 1600 and 1700 cm−1 and near 1600 cm−1, respectively. TTA-Si were dominated by the very intense anti-symmetric v as (Si–O) stretching vibrations at approximately 1100 cm−1. The bands at 1628 cm−1 in the spectra of the TTA-Si were ascribed to stretching vibrations of the –CONH-groups of TTA-Si [17]. Most likely due to Ln3+ coordination, the peak for the –CONH-group was much weaker in the spectrum of Tb(TTA-Si)3. FTIR spectroscopy showed that it is feasible to dissolve chitosan in CD solution and that both TTA and CDs can coordinate with Tb3+.

Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis is particularly useful for observing thermal decomposition of hybrid materials. TG curves of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS are shown in Fig. 2E. The first weight loss below 150 °C was caused by loss of coordinated water. The second weight loss occurred between 230 and 550 °C, and the peak levelled off as the temperature was increased, which was attributed to decomposition of organic species. The total weight loss for Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS was approximately 88.94%.

The CDs-CS solution remained clear after the CS dissolved, and blue luminescence was emitted under 365 nm irradiation. The effects of different components in the sensor were explained. The CDs solution was used to dissolve chitosan, and TTA was used to coordinate with Tb3+. The combination of TTA and Si can improve the solubility of the lanthanide complex, and the carboxyl group in the CDs solution can also coordinate withTb3+. The solution of the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS emitted green luminescence under 365 nm irradiation, while the film of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS emitted bright green luminescence under the same conditions. After dialysis, the pH of the CD solution was approximately 2, and after the CS was dissolved, the pH increased to approximately 5 due to interactions between H+ and amino groups. However, a weakly acidic environment is not conducive to luminescence by Tb3+, so the green fluorescence of the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS solution was weak. Moreover, the interaction was further confirmed by the more negative ζ-potential of CDs (+18.6 mV) compared to that of CDs-CS (+6.8 mV). The zeta potential values of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS was + 11.6 mV. After the film was prepared, a small amount of acid (incomplete reaction phenyl boronic acid) was volatilized, and the luminescent group was fixed on the film, so the energy transfer efficiency improved and the luminescence was enhanced.

UV − vis and luminescence spectroscopy illustrated the optical properties of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CD-CS (Fig. 4). The peak at 230 nm is the characteristic absorption of the CDs. The enhanced absorption peak at 285 nm in the spectrum of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS involved an n − π* transition and confirmed the interaction between Tb3+ and CDs. Tb3+ disturbed the energy bands of the CDs [18]. The positions of the absorption peaks for CDs and CS remained consistent, indicating that acid CDs solution can dissolve CS and maintain the original luminescence properties of the CDs. The bands from 295 to 350 nm in the Fig. 4B of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS are assigned to absorptions of TTA ligands [19]. The wide peak ranging from 295-350 nm in Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS spectrum indicates that the hybrid material combined the absorption peaks of TTA and CDs.

The luminescent properties of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS were evaluated with excitation and emission spectra. It should be noted that chitosan and carbon dots solution have no solvent effect. The excitation spectra of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS solutions exhibited an excitation band with two peaks at 295 nm and 350 nm, which could be assigned to the CDs and TTA (Fig. 4B). The emission spectrum of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS shown in Fig. 3
C displayed four sharp lines at 490, 550, 583 and 618 nm ascribed to 5D4→7F3, 5D4→7F4, 5D4→7F5 and 5D4→7F6 transitions, with the 5D4→7F5 transition at 550 nm being the most prominent one; this transition was responsible for the green emission [20]. Successful antenna effects have also been confirmed for organic ligands and Ln3+, and the ability of CDs to provide sufficient photophysical effects to achieve efficient lanthanide photosensitization is encouraging [21].

As observed by the luminescence images (Figure S1), the CD-CS solution exhibited blue luminescence upon irradiation with ultraviolet light (365 nm). The Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS solution exhibited bright green luminescence induced with ultraviolet light (365 nm). The emission of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS was observed to be stable after storage for 21 days (storage: room temperature, sunlight), which implied that the emission would not exhibit significant decay and was suitable for further sensing in real samples (Figure S2).

As mentioned above, Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS is soluble in water and retains a certain consistency. Hence, these complexes might form homogeneous films. The flexible, luminescent films were prepared simply by drying at room temperature. In this hybrid material, the amino group combined with protons from the CDs to form a viscous homogeneous aqueous solution. The CDs retained the original carboxylic acid groups through the hydrothermal treatment and coordinated with Tb3+ by combining with TTA, thus forming a luminescent film. CDs play important roles in connecting various substances. Furthermore, the films of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS could also be used as sensors for VOC detection.

The output luminescence intensity of the sensor gradually decreased upon increasing the formic acid fraction (Fig. 5 A), and a noticeable response was observed when the formic acid fraction was decreased to as low as 0.01% (v/v). Additionally, the formic acid fraction and the response intensity were linearly related in the range 0.01–0.12% (Fig. 5B). Compared with reported sensors, the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CD-CS probe exhibited higher sensitivity and much stronger potential for FA detection.

Formic acid, the simplest organic carboxylic acid, has both carboxylic and aldehyde groups in its molecular structure, so it is both acidic and reducing. As shown in Fig. 5, the luminescence intensity of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS gradually decreased with increasing FA concentration. Hence, we examined the effects of VOCs on the luminescence of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS. As shown in Fig. 6 , volatilized FA induced significant luminescence quenching, while no remarkable changes in the luminescence of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS were produced by other volatilized substances. Thus, Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS was used for FA detection in a complex system owing to the excellent quenching caused specifically by FA.

The mechanism for the response of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS luminescence to FA was studied in detail. In the process of making the film, water molecules evaporated, and formic acid molecules combined with hydrophilic groups on the surface of the film as they entered the film; however, formic acid did not damage the existing skeleton structure of the film as it entered. We next investigated the optical properties of the film in the presence and absence of formic acid vapor. A thin film of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS was prepared on a quartz slide, and its absorbance was measured using UV–vis spectroscopy. An increase in absorbance was observed as formic acid vapor flowed through the membrane (Fig. S4), indicating that the membrane absorbed more light in the presence of formic acid vapor. This may be due to interactions between polar formic acid molecules and hydrophilic functional groups on the membrane surface and/or the solvent color effect, which changes the optical properties that can be monitored by UV–vis spectroscopy. The change in optical properties of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS upon exposure to FA could be due to a change in polarization around the CDs produced by the solvent molecules. The dipole–dipole interactions and hydrogen bonding between the CDs and formic acid explain the changes in absorption [22]. FTIR spectra of the film before and after exposure to FA vapor are shown in Fig. 3, which shows that the peaks at approximately 3200 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 were stronger than those of the untreated film. These two peaks correspond to carboxylic acid groups, and the luminescence of Ln3+ was obviously quenched in an acidic environment [23]. The CDs made of carboxylic acid were better adapted to the environment, and the change in blue light intensity at 450 nm was weak (Fig. S5). Formic acid has an aldehyde group and carboxyl group, and studies indicated that acetic acid and acetaldehyde did not have luminescence quenching abilities similar to those of FA. These results provide evidence for selective detection of FA through synergistic effects, including polar effects, acidity and reducing properties. Therefore, Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS possesses great potential for sensing FA in chemical production.

We investigated the response time of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS to FA. The peak at 550 nm gradually decreased over time, by 80% within five minutes (Figure S3). This result showed that the film responds quickly to FA. The sensing reversibility was examined by the luminescence emission intensity at 550 nm, we treated Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS film with FA vapor and Et3N gas alternately for several FA-Et3N cycles, and the results are shown in Figure S6. This film displays good reversibility after 5 cycles. We believe that Et3N can neutralization the acidic sites on pellets of HN embedded within CS films. The overall (absolute) quantum yields of the Tb(TTA-Si)3 and Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS were determined with a calibrated sphere system and were found to be 0.45 and 0.31, respectively. This result indicates that the Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS is beneficial for the energy-transfer efficiency from the ligand to Tb3+ ions, despite the negative influence on the quantum yield and the radiative transition process. Compared with other formic acid detection methods, lanthanide luminescence detection has higher sensitivity and faster response [5,24–25].

In summary, a film-based luminescent sensor for detection of formic acid vapor with high performance has been realized. The film was prepared by dissolving chitosan in an acidic CD solution. Owing to the properties of the film, a luminescent sensor with high sensitivity and a wide response range for formic acid vapor was developed. The sensor exhibited high selectivity for formic acid vapor over water and some commonly used volatile compounds, and the response of Tb(TTA-Si)3@CDs-CS to formic acid was not affected when the response intensity was used for analysis. The fast response (5 min), high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and outstanding environmental protection capability of the sensor laid a solid foundation for further application. With this sensor, the testing process does not produce secondary contamination. The volume fraction of formic acid detectable by the film was lower than 0.01% (v/v). This unique membrane and developed sensor will undoubtedly contribute to detection of formic acid with applications in corrosion protection, health protection, product quality control and environmental monitoring.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2021208021). The authors would like to thank Yingchun Li from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2023.05.023.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Moonrinta, B. Kwon, I. In, S. Kladsomboon, W. Sajomsang, P. Paoprasert, Highly biocompatible yogurt-derived carbon dots as multipurpose sensors for detection of formic acid vapor and metal ions, Optical Materials. 81 (2018) 93-101.
S. Lin, T.M. Swager, Carbon Nanotube Formic Acid Sensors Using a Nickel Bis (ortho-diiminosemiquinonate) Selector, ACS Sens. 3 (2018) 569-73.

A. Kumar, A. Kumar, P.R. Sahoo, S. Kumar, A MC-spiropyran for smartphone assisted reversible, selective and nanomolar level detection of formic acid in water and gas phase, J. Mol. Struct. 1223 (2021) 129249.
N. Tammanoon, A. Wisitsoraat, A. Tuantranont, C. Liewhiran, Flame-made Zn-substituted SnO2 nanoparticulate compound for ultra-sensitive formic acid gas sensing, J. Alloys. Compd. 871 (2021) 159547.

Y. Wu, C. Hua, Z. Liu, J. Yang, R. Huang, M. Li, K. Liu, R. Miao, Y. Fang, High-Performance Sensing of Formic Acid Vapor Enabled by a Newly Developed Nanofilm-Based Fluorescent Sensor, Anal. Chem. 93 (2021) 7094-101.
Z. Yuan, C. Yang, H. Gao, W. Qin, F. Meng, High Response Formic Acid Gas Sensor Based on MoS2 Nanosheets, IEEE T. Nanotechnol. 20 (2021) 177-184.

Z. Xia, C. Zheng, J. Hu, Q. Yuan, C. Zhang, J. Zhang, L. He, H. Gao, L. Jin, X. Chu, F. Meng, Synthesis of SnO2 quantum dot sensitized LaFeO3 for conductometric formic acid gas sensors, Sensor Actuat. B-Chem. 379 (2023) 133198
S. Takeshita, S. Zhao, W.J. Malfait, M.M. Koebel, Chemistry of Chitosan Aerogels: Three-Dimensional Pore Control for Tailored Applications, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 60 (2021) 9828-51.
E. Lizundia, T.D. Nguyen, R.J. Winnick, M.J. MacLachlan, Biomimetic photonic materials derived from chitin and chitosan, J. Mater. Chem. C 9 (2021) 796-817.

Y. Li, M. Sun, Y. Yang, H. Meng, Q. Wang, C. Li, G. Li, Luminescence-colour-changing sensing toward neurological drug carbamazepine in water and biofluids based on white light-emitting CD/Ln-MOF/PVA test papers, J. Mater. Chem. C 9 (2021) 8683-93.
C. Ji, Y. Zhou, R.M. Leblanc, Z. Peng, Recent Developments of Carbon Dots in Biosensing: A Review, ACS Sens. 5 (2020) 2724-41.
J. Min, X.L. Qu, B. Yan, Tb post-functionalized La (III) metal organic framework hybrid probe for simple and highly sensitive detection of acetaldehyde, Sens. Actuators B 300 (2019) 126985.
P. Zhang, F. Zhao, W. Shi, H. Lu, X. Zhou, Y. Guo, G. Yu, Super Water-Extracting Gels for Solar-Powered Volatile Organic Compounds Management in the Hydrological Cycle, Adv. Mater. 34 (2022) 2110548.
Y. Zhao, H. Zeng, X.W. Zhu, W. Lu, D. Li, Metal-organic frameworks as photoluminescent biosensing platforms: mechanisms and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 50 (2021) 4484-513.

Y. J. Li, X. D. Yu, T. Yu, Eu3+ based mesoporous hybrid material with tunable multicolor emission modulated by fluoride ion: application for selective sensing toward fluoride ion. J. Mater. Chem. C, 5 (2017) 5411--5419
